perturbation.original_text: Pursuant to the Agreement to which this Exhibit B is annexed, Company may be disclosing to Independent Contractor certain confidential business plans, development plans, reports, financial information, design documents, specifications, programmer notes, software (its own and/or third party), and/or other information, whether or not so identified (together with any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents that are based upon, contain, or otherwise reflect such information, the "Confidential Information," which shall include this Agreement).
perturbation.changed_text: Pursuant to the Agreement to which this Exhibit B is annexed, Company may be disclosing to Independent Contractor certain information, whether or not so identified (together with any notes, analyses, compilations, studies, or other documents that are based upon, contain, or otherwise reflect such information, the "Confidential Information," which shall include this Agreement).
perturbation.explanation: By removing specific examples of 'confidential business plans, development plans, reports, financial information, design documents, specifications, programmer notes, software (its own and/or third party),' the definition of 'Confidential Information' becomes vague. Section 4 requires the Independent Contractor to redeliver 'all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company'. With the examples removed from the initial definition, it's unclear whether certain documents or information are truly considered 'Confidential Information,' creating an in-text contradiction and making enforcement of the redelivery obligation in Section 4 uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by the Company to Independent Contractor shall be and shall remain the Company's property. Upon termination of the Agreement, Independent Contractor shall redeliver all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company. Except to the extent Independent Contractor is advised in writing by counsel that such action is prohibited by law, Independent Contractor will also destroy all written material, memoranda, notes, and other writings or recordings whatsoever prepared by it based upon, containing, or otherwise reflecting any Confidential Information. Any Confidential Information that is not returned or destroyed, including without limitation any oral Confidential Information, shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations set forth in this Exhibit B.
perturbation.justification: The change removes specific examples of 'Confidential Information'. Section 4 requires redelivery of 'all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company.' With the removal of the examples, it is now unclear whether certain documents or information are considered 'Confidential Information', making the enforcement of the redelivery obligation in Section 4 uncertain, and therefore creates an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In fulfilling its obligations under this Agreement, Independent Contractor shall use at least the same standard of care it uses to protect its own information of similar kind, but not less than a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.changed_text: In fulfilling its obligations under this Agreement, Independent Contractor shall use a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'at least the same standard of care it uses to protect its own information of similar kind' lowers the standard of care required of the Independent Contractor. If the Independent Contractor does not meticulously safeguard the Company's confidential information, relying solely on a 'reasonable standard of care,' it may lead to breaches that Section 5, which provides for injunctive relief and other remedies for any breach of Exhibit B, may become hard to be enforced, creating uncertainty and a potential contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Independent Contractor acknowledges and agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Exhibit B by Independent Contractor and that the Company shall be entitled to specific performance, including without limitation injunctive relief, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for breach of this Exhibit B but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity. Independent Contractor agrees to reimburse the Company for costs and expenses (including without limitation attorneys' fees) incurred by the Company in connection with the enforcement of this Exhibit B.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a higher standard of care ('at least the same standard of care it uses to protect its own information of similar kind, but not less than a reasonable standard of care.'). The changed text reduces this to only 'a reasonable standard of care'. The contradicted text states that money damages may not be sufficient and that the Company is entitled to injunctive relief for any breach of Exhibit B. By lowering the standard of care, the Independent Contractor's actions could more easily lead to a breach that is not easily remedied, making the injunctive relief clause less effective and creating a contradiction. This aligns with the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' definition where 'essential language is removed or altered in one section, creating a contradiction with obligations or constraints stated in other sections.'

perturbation.original_text: The term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed not to include information which (i) is or becomes generally available to the public other than (a) as a result of a disclosure by Independent Contractor or any other person who directly or indirectly receives such information from the Independent Contractor or (b) in violation of a confidentiality obligation to the Company known to Independent Contractor or (ii) is or becomes available to Independent Contractor on a non-confidential basis from a source which is entitled to disclose it to Independent Contractor or (iii) is independently developed by Independent Contractor without benefit of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Confidential Information" shall be deemed not to include information which (i) is or becomes generally available to the public or (ii) is or becomes available to Independent Contractor on a non-confidential basis from a source which is entitled to disclose it to Independent Contractor or (iii) is independently developed by Independent Contractor without benefit of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'other than (a) as a result of a disclosure by Independent Contractor or any other person who directly or indirectly receives such information from the Independent Contractor or (b) in violation of a confidentiality obligation to the Company known to Independent Contractor' from the exclusion clause, information leaked by the contractor would not be considered confidential, thus allowing for the leaks, but at the same time, it will contradict section 1 and 5 that refer to keeping the information as confidential and not doing breaches. Also affecting Section 4 in case of termination and how that should be.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Independent Contractor shall use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of performing the Services specified in the applicable SOW and not for any other purpose. Except to the extent permitted by Section 3 below, Independent Contractor will not disclose the Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any other party. In fulfilling its obligations under this Agreement, Independent Contractor shall use a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the exception for disclosures by the Independent Contractor or those receiving information from them, thus expanding the definition of non-confidential information. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which obligates the Independent Contractor to not disclose the Confidential Information, creating a clear conflict and therefore a strong in-text contradiction. The explanation provided details this contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Independent Contractor is required by law or by interrogatories, requests for information or documents, subpoena, Civil Investigative Demand, or similar process to disclose any information supplied to Independent Contractor pursuant to the Agreement, including without limitation the Confidential Information or any other information the disclosure of which is restricted by the terms of this Exhibit B, Independent Contractor will provide the Company with prompt prior written notice of such request or requirement so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Independent Contractor is required by law to disclose any information supplied to Independent Contractor pursuant to the Agreement, Independent Contractor will provide the Company with prompt prior written notice of such request or requirement so that the Company may seek an appropriate protective order.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'or by interrogatories, requests for information or documents, subpoena, Civil Investigative Demand, or similar process,' the scope of situations requiring notice is narrowed. This contradicts the overall intention of the agreement, as other forms of legal pressure to disclose information, especially confidential information, are now excluded. This affects section 1 where they commit to not disclosing this information, and the legal process not being considered here now will allow the Indepent Contractor to do so.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Independent Contractor shall use the Confidential Information solely for the purpose of performing the Services specified in the applicable SOW and not for any other purpose. Except to the extent permitted by Section 3 below, Independent Contractor will not disclose the Confidential Information, in whole or in part, to any other party. In fulfilling its obligations under this Agreement, Independent Contractor shall use a reasonable standard of care.
perturbation.justification: The original text required Independent Contractor to notify the Company when compelled to disclose information due to various legal processes, including interrogatories, requests for information, subpoenas, etc. The changed text narrows this obligation by only requiring notice when compelled by law. This creates a contradiction with the 'Contradicted Text,' which states that the Independent Contractor will not disclose Confidential Information, since the narrowed scope no longer covers scenarios where disclosure is required by a process other than 'law.' The change permits disclosure in scenarios previously restricted, which contradicts the section committing to not disclose confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: Except to the extent Independent Contractor is advised in writing by counsel that such action is prohibited by law, Independent Contractor will also destroy all written material, memoranda, notes, and other writings or recordings whatsoever prepared by it based upon, containing, or otherwise reflecting any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor will also destroy all written material, memoranda, notes, and other writings or recordings whatsoever prepared by it based upon, containing, or otherwise reflecting any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'Except to the extent Independent Contractor is advised in writing by counsel that such action is prohibited by law,' a blanket requirement to destroy all confidential information-related materials is created. This creates a contradiction if local legislation is in place to store the data. With that said, section 4 will contradict the fact of deleting the documents in case local legislation is in place to store this data.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed by the Company to Independent Contractor shall be and shall remain the Company's property. Upon termination of the Agreement, Independent Contractor shall redeliver all tangible Confidential Information furnished by the Company. Independent Contractor will also destroy all written material, memoranda, notes, and other writings or recordings whatsoever prepared by it based upon, containing, or otherwise reflecting any Confidential Information. Any Confidential Information that is not returned or destroyed, including without limitation any oral Confidential Information, shall remain subject to the confidentiality obligations set forth in this Exhibit B.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the Independent Contractor to refrain from destroying confidential information if advised by counsel that such action is prohibited by law. The changed text removes this exception, creating a blanket requirement to destroy all such information. This contradicts the contradicted text, which outlines the procedure for handling confidential information and implies that destruction may not always be required if certain confidentiality obligations are maintained. The change makes the contractor liable for not destroying the confidential information even if local legislation requires them to keep it, triggering a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Independent Contractor agrees to reimburse the Company for costs and expenses (including without limitation attorneys' fees) incurred by the Company in connection with the enforcement of this Exhibit B.
perturbation.changed_text: Independent Contractor agrees to reimburse the Company for costs and expenses incurred by the Company in connection with disputes related to this Exhibit B.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'enforcement' with 'disputes', the obligation to reimburse legal fees is broadened but simultaneously weakened. It now covers all disputes, not just those relating to enforcement. But if there is no action of enforcement, this section would be contradict in itself. It no longer is related to enforcement because the modified sentence covers all disputes, even those where no actual breach requiring enforcement occurred, which introduces an element of ambiguity, making it hard to enforce that section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Independent Contractor acknowledges and agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Exhibit B by Independent Contractor and that the Company shall be entitled to specific performance, including without limitation injunctive relief, as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedy shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedy for breach of this Exhibit B but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity. Independent Contractor agrees to reimburse the Company for costs and expenses incurred by the Company in connection with disputes related to this Exhibit B.
perturbation.justification: The original text limited reimbursement to costs associated with 'enforcement' of Exhibit B, whereas the changed text broadens it to 'disputes related to this Exhibit B.' The contradicted text, which now includes this broadened reimbursement obligation, discusses remedies for 'breach' of Exhibit B. The change introduces a contradiction because the reimbursement obligation now applies even when there's no breach, but simply a 'dispute', which the original agreement did not explicitly cover. The contradicted text focuses on breach and remedies, which now conflicts with the broader scope of 'disputes,' creating a strong in-text contradiction, based on the definition of Omissions - In Text Contradiction.
